name: 15 mi. NW of Kingsman
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site near Kingman with excellent agate and jasper nodules in desert washes. Free collecting with 25 lb daily limit, beginner-friendly terrain.
description:
This remote BLM site sits on the high desert plateau northwest of Kingman in Mohave County, offering productive rockhounding in an open, sparsely vegetated landscape. The area is characterized by moderate slopes and scattered desert scrub, with nearby washes including Left Fork Vock Canyon and Vock Canyon providing good collecting zones where erosion exposes colorful specimens.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province geology, where ancient volcanic and sedimentary deposits have weathered to release nodules and fragments of agate, jasper, and chalcedony. The silica-rich host rocks have been broken down by millions of years of freeze-thaw cycles and water erosion, concentrating gem material in accessible washes and surface deposits.

Collecting here requires no permit and follows standard BLM personal-use rules. The terrain is moderate elevation desert with good walking conditions in most areas, though you should scout the washes carefully as they can contain loose rock. The open landscape provides little shade, so plan for exposure.

This location is best suited for rockhounders comfortable with remote backcountry access and willing to scan wash gravels and hillside exposures carefully. Experienced collectors often find quality specimens in stream gravels after seasonal runoff, while novices can spot surface nodules fairly easily in the scattered talus.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic activity and subsequent weathering have created extensive deposits of silica-rich nodules and fragments. The nearby Vock Canyon system has carried eroded material from higher elevation sources, concentrating agate and jasper in accessible wash gravels over millions of years.
tips: Search the bottoms and banks of Left Fork Vock Canyon and Vock Canyon first, as gravity and water flow concentrate heavier specimens. Look for brightly colored nodules ranging from 1 to 4 inches - reds, oranges, and browns are common in this area. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and dig bar to work loose material in wash gravels; the compacted soil here often requires some effort to extract specimens. Early morning visits are essential in summer to avoid midday heat exposure on this exposed plateau.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4387 ft with moderate slopes and scattered low desert vegetation including creosote and palo verde. Relatively open terrain with good visibility and walking conditions, though washes contain loose rock and require careful footing. Parking pullout to collecting areas is typically 0.5 to 2 miles on foot, mostly on exposed ground with minimal shade.
best_season: October - April. Winter temperatures are mild (40-60°F), and fall rains improve erosion in nearby washes, exposing fresh material. Summer heat (100-115°F) is extreme at this elevation with no water sources nearby, making June-September challenging.
written_directions: From Kingman, take US 66 (Andy Devine Avenue) west approximately 4 miles to Pierce Ferry Road. Continue north on Pierce Ferry Road for about 11-12 miles toward the BLM lands northwest of Kingman. Watch for marked BLM access points and dispersed camping areas. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended for the final rough access roads. GPS coordinates 35.403312, -114.175229 mark the general collection area. Local BLM office maps and detailed topographic maps are essential for pinpointing exact entry points.
road_conditions: Rough graded dirt roads requiring high-clearance vehicle. Washboard surface common. Final approach roads may be impassable after rain.
parking_notes: Dispersed parking at pullouts along access roads. No developed lot. Space for 2-4 vehicles at most pullouts. BLM dispersed camping allowed.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Collectors must pack out all trash. Respect all closures and stay on BLM public land.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Pack out all trash
Stay on BLM land only
nearest_services: Kingman, Arizona (15 miles southeast) has all services including fuel, food, water, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with open desert walking and some wash scrambling. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for loose rock navigation. Limited shade and water access - bring 3+ liters per person. Not recommended for those with heat sensitivity or limited mobility.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find 15 miles northwest of Kingman?
faq.answer: This BLM area produces colorful nodular agate and jasper in reds, oranges, browns, and whites. Most specimens range 1-4 inches in diameter. Look for banded and brecciated varieties in wash gravels, particularly after seasonal runoff enriches the exposed material.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound on BLM land near Kingman, Arizona?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal collecting on this BLM site. You only need to follow the 25 lb per day limit under FLPMA regulations and ensure all collecting is for personal use, not commercial.

faq.question: How far is the rockhounding area from the nearest parking, and what vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: The collecting areas are 0.5 to 2 miles from parking pullouts along the access roads. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is strongly recommended due to rough graded dirt roads and washboard conditions. Passenger cars may not clear the final approach.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding northwest of Kingman?
faq.answer: October through April offers ideal conditions. Winter temperatures stay mild (40-60°F), and fall rains improve wash erosion that exposes fresh material. Summer heat (100-115°F) is dangerous on this exposed plateau with minimal shade and no water sources.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to find agates at this remote BLM site?
faq.answer: Bring a dig bar or small pry bar for working compacted wash gravels, a 5-gallon bucket, and a hand lens for identifying specimens. Most importantly, carry 3+ liters of water per person, sunscreen, and a hat. This is remote desert with no services within 15 miles.

faq.question: Is this Kingman rockhounding site beginner-friendly, and can families with kids go there?
faq.answer: Yes, it is beginner-friendly with visible surface nodules easy to spot in washes and talus slopes. Families with kids ages 8+ can succeed here, though young children need close supervision due to heat exposure, remote location, and loose rock hazards. Plan short trips in cooler months.

meta_title: 15 mi NW Kingman Agate Rockhounding - Arizona BLM
meta_description: Free rockhounding near Kingman, AZ. Collect agate, jasper, and quartz on BLM land. 25 lb daily limit, no permit. October-April.
